answer:
Iqbal's representation of the sun (Aaftab, or dawn's herald) in *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* is far more theological than the Old English Elegies' incidental mentions of dawn.[^1] 

In "Aaftab," the sun is exalted as a divine emanation: the "world's own soul," the cause of existence emerging "from Unbeing," the source of life's "urge" and "harmony," equated with "Heart, is Mind, is Soul, is conscious Light," and invoked to "bestow" the "light of consciousness" upon humanity while "mak[ing] our eye of reason... glow."[^2] It is the eternal "Lord" furnishing all creation, embodying "perfection... in every living heart," "defying death," and transcending time—explicitly metaphysical and panentheistic in Iqbal's Islamic philosophical mode.[^3]

By contrast, dawn in the *Old English Elegies* (e.g., *The Wanderer*, *The Seafarer*) functions naturalistically as a temporal marker of existential sorrow and transience, not a theological symbol. In *The Wanderer*, the speaker must "alone, at each dawn, lament my sorrows," awakening to "fallow waves, sea-birds... frost and snow," where memory renews "heart's wounds" amid wyrd's decay.[^4] Similarly, *The Seafarer* frames dawn within "paths of exile," "night-shadows," and "snow... from the north," spurring longing but pivoting to Christian hope only abstractly ("joys of the Lord... than this dead life").[^5] No elegy deifies dawn or sun; it underscores mutability ("middle-earth... droops and decays"), contrasting worldly loss with heavenly stability, but dawn remains phenomenological.[^6]

Iqbal theologizes dawn as God's immanent light and life-force; the Elegies use it elegiacally to evoke wyrd-bound grief, with theology as eschatological remedy.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc). Full chapters: Iqbal [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/); Elegies representative [/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/](works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 14 "Aaftab" [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-fdcce3).

[^3]: Ibid., [paragraphs](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-af377c) and [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-d0845f](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-d0845f).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 "The Wanderer" [paragraphs](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^5]: Ibid., ch. 5 "The Seafarer" [paragraphs](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-889339) and [/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-eff626](works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/05-maey-ich-be-me-sylfum-soth-yied-wrekan-sithas-seya/translation/#p-eff626).

[^6]: See also ch. 3 [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-c1cc32); corpus-wide, no dawn theology in Elegies (search: dawn/morning/sunrise yielded 0 direct hits).
